coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of blue fluorescent molybdenum nanoclusters with novel terephthaldehyde-cysteine Schiff base for detection of pyrophosphate
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kateshiya, Mehul R.
Malek, Naved I.
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: In this work, terephthaldehyde-cysteine-molybdenum nanoclusters (TPA-Cys-MoNCs) were synthesized by using terephthaldehyde-cysteine (TPA-Cys) Schiff base as a novel ligand. The as-synthesized TPA-Cys-MoNCs showed blue fluorescence under UV lamp at 365 nm, displaying emission peak at 455 nm when excited at 340 nm. The fluorescent TPA-Cys-MoNCs are used as a probe for sensitive assay of pyrophosphate (PPi) via fluorescence quenching mechanism. The emission peak intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs at 455 nm exhibited a linear quenching with increasing amount of PPi. As a result, quantitative assay was developed for the detection of PPi (0.01–200 µM) with the detection limit of 0.9 nM. The developed probe was successfully demonstrated for the detection of PPi in biofluids (urine and plasma).
coredata.dcterms:subject: TPA-Cys-MoNCs
Fluorescence assay
Pyrophosphate
HR-TEM and biofluids

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India ( EMR/2016/002621/IPC ). MK sincerely acknowledges the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India for providing the doctoral fellowship to carry this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–visible spectra, fluorescence excitation (at 340 nm) and emission (at 455 nm) spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs (Inset: Photographic image of TPA-Cys-MoNCs under UV lamp at 365 nm and daylight).

HR-TEM images of (a) TPA-Cys-MoNCs at 5 nm scale bar, (b) lattice fringes of TPA-Cys-MoNCs at 2 nm scale bar, (c) TPA-Cys-MoNCs + PPi at 5 nm scale bar. Histograms of (d) TPA-Cys-MoNCs at 5 nm scale bar and (e) TPA-Cys-MoNCs + PPi at 5 nm scale bar.
XPS data of TPA-Cys-MoNCs, (a) full-scan spectra (Mo, S, C, N, and O), High-resolution spectra of (b) Mo 3d and (c) S 2p.

Investigation of fluorescence intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs with the addition of different biomolecules (PPi, ALP, ATP, AMP, adenine, cytidine, guanine, BSA, GSH and thymine, 1 mM) and (Inset: Photograph of above solution (1) PPi, (2) ALP, (3) ATP, (4) AMP, (5) adenine, (6) cytidine, (7) guanine, (8) BSA, (9) GSH and (10) thymine under a UV lamp at 365 nm).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs at different concentrations of PPi (0.010–200 µM) Inset: Photograph of different concentrations of PPi under a UV lamp at 365 nm). (b) Calibration curve between I0/I and concentration of PPi (0.010–200 µM).
Schematic representation for the synthesis of TPA-Cys-MoNCs by using TPA-Cys Schiff base as a ligand and their sensing application for assaying of PPi.
Comparison of analytical performance of TPA-Cys-MoNCs-based analytical approach for the detection of PPi with reported methods in the literature.
Assaying of PPi in biological (blood plasma and urine) samples using TPA-Cys-MoNCs as a fluorescent probe.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of blue fluorescent molybdenum nanoclusters with novel terephthaldehyde-cysteine Schiff base for detection of pyrophosphate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallbhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallbhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallbhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, terephthaldehyde-cysteine-molybdenum nanoclusters (TPA-Cys-MoNCs) were synthesized by using terephthaldehyde-cysteine (TPA-Cys) Schiff base as a novel ligand. The as-synthesized TPA-Cys-MoNCs showed blue fluorescence under UV lamp at 365 nm, displaying emission peak at 455 nm when excited at 340 nm. The fluorescent TPA-Cys-MoNCs are used as a probe for sensitive assay of pyrophosphate (PPi) via fluorescence quenching mechanism. The emission peak intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs at 455 nm exhibited a linear quenching with increasing amount of PPi. As a result, quantitative assay was developed for the detection of PPi (0.01–200 µM) with the detection limit of 0.9 nM. The developed probe was successfully demonstrated for the detection of PPi in biofluids (urine and plasma).

ce:section.ce:para: In analytical science, colorimetry is one of the simple and straightforward analytical tool for the identification of analytes via measuring absorbance/transmittance/reflectance.. Optical sensors phenomena are based on the chemical interactions of chemoresponsive molecules/materials with the target analtyes in the presence of light (ultraviolet, visible or infrared), thereby favoring drastic changes in optical characteristics (absorbance, reflectance, diffraction, scattering and fluorescence), which helps to establish analytical platfrom for quantitative measurement of target analytes. In recent years, functional metal nanoclusters (NCs) are emerging as promising probes for chemical sensing applications, offering remarkable analytical features in assaying of wide variety chemical species [1]. With the blossom of functional metal NCs, fluorescent metal NCs-based optical sensors have created a new path for the development of a simple, and cost-effective analytical device for the selective, and real-time detection of trace level target analytes [2]. Metal NCs armed with discrete electronic structures, thereby providing molecule-like properties (quantized charging, electronic transitions (HOMO − LUMO - highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital), molecular chirality and magnetism), which offers new opportunities in optical sensing [3,4]. Organic ligands are widely used for the synthesis of metal NCs because of various features such as active functional groups, generation of NCs with precise number, high stability, superior quantum yield (QY) and excellent surface chemistry [5]. Therefore, ligand chemistry has emerged as a new study topic for understanding the structure and characteristics of metal NCs, as well as the development of metal NCs-based miniaturized analytical strategies for recognition of trace level analytes [6]. Various metal NCs such as gold [7–9], copper [10,11], silver [12,13] and platinum [14,15] have been fabricated and used as optical sensors for selective identification of various molecules (organic- spermine, indapamide, dopamine, carbidopa, flutamide, chloramphenicol Co2+, Cu2+, and Hg2+ ions) from complex samples. It was observed that molybdenum NCs (MoNCs) have shown high biocompatibility as compared to gold, silver, and platinum owing to their association with biomacromolecules [16]. Thiolate ligands have been frequently used as ligands for the preparation of metal NCs with thermodynamic stability and tunable surface properties [17]. The introduction of organic molecules as ligands plays key role in controlling the number of atoms in metal NCs and in turning their surface chemistry, favoring them to act as selective sensors for the identification of trace level chemical species.

Inorganic pyrophosphate (P2O7 4-, PPi) is a dimer that plays a key role in various biological and ecological activities [18]. It acts as an important molecule in various biological reactions including hydrolysis of adenosine triphosphate disodium salt (ATP), polymerization of nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) [19–21] and other metabolic processes [22]. PPi has been recognised as a key element in controlling calcium metabolism; consequently, a modest amount of PPi inhibits the precipitation of calcium phosphate from solution and binds strongly to hydroxyapatite crystals [22,23]. It was noticed that PPi-treated apatite crystals are easily developed and disintegrated more slowly than untreated crystals. It acted as a biomarker for the monitoring of various disease progress such as arthritis, cancer, crystal deposition and infectious diseases [24], revealing the concentration of PPi in biofluids could be used as biomarker for various diseases monitoring. For example, abnormal high concentration of PPi in synovial fluid is due to the crystal deposition of calcium pyrophosphate dehydration [25]. Therefore, PPi plays a key role in the pathological process of arthritis, demonstrating it could be employed as a biomarker for the clinical diagnosis and treatment of arthritis [26], which confirms that the selective assay of PPi in biofluids provides significant information for the diagnosis of various diseases. Various analytical methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography [27], chemiluminescence [28], electrochemical [29], colorimetry [30,31], and molecular sensors [21] have been applied for assaying of PPi in biological fluids. Although these approaches have significantly improved the sensitivity and selectivity for PPi, unfortunately they are sophisticated instruments, laborious and required toxic and expensive solvents. Due to the selectivity, sensitivity and simplicity of metal NCs-based optical sensors, metal NCs-based fluorescence assay is recognized as one of the promising analytical tool for recognition of various target molecules [32,33]. Importantly, pioneering works on sensing of PPi and other inorganic species by several research groups [21,34], the metal NCs-based fluorescence assay of PPi in biological fluids has drawn significant interest. Further, functional metal NCs-based fluorescent sensors exhibited outstanding optical properties and surface chemistry, thereby enabling sensitive assay of target analytes with improved selectivity. Among the used ligands for the synthesis of metal NCs, Schiff base derivatives are considered as promising ligands for functionalization of nanostructure materials due to their features such as active functional groups, and high affinity to bind with metal nanostructures. Inspired by the above works, a promising terephthaldehyde-cysteine (TPA-Cys) Schiff base ligand was synthesized by the reaction between terephthaldehyde (TPA) and cysteine (Cys) and used for the preparation of molybdenum nanoclusters (TPA-Cys-MoNCs). Importantly, TPA-Cys Shiff base can also display high ability to bind with precise number of Mo3+ ions, thereby allowing controlled reduction of Mo3+ ions to Mo0, which favors to tune their optical properties. Further, encapsulation of MoNCs with TPA-Cys Shiff base derivative can offer unqiue features to interact with PPi through various covalent and non-covalent interactions.

Herein, TPA-Cys-MoNCs were synthesized by using MoCl3 as a precursor and TPA-Cys Schiff base as a ligand. The as-fabricated TPA-Cys Schiff base is having free thiol group that can easily encapsulate Mo3+ ion for the controlled reduction from Mo3+ ion to Mo2+ ion and Mo0, thereby generating blue emissive TPA-Cys-MoNCs. The as-fabricated TPA-Cys-MoNCs exhibited an intense emission peak at 455 nm when excited at 340 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs at 455 nm was almost disappeared with the addition of PPi. The emission ratio of TAP-Cys-MoNCs (I0/I, I0 is the intensity without PPi and I is the intensity with PPi) exhibits a linear response over PPi concentration (0.01–200 µM), offering the detection limit of 0.9 nM, which signifies that the developed probe exhibits several analytical features rapidity, sensitivity and selectivity for the analysis of PPi. The TPA-Cys-MoNCs-based fluorescence method could be used as a facile and rapid analytical platform for the analysis of PPi in biofluids (urine and plasma).

Molybdenum(III) chloride (MoCl3) (13478-18-7, molecular weight (MW) −202.30 Da), all biomolecules, PPi (13472-36-1, MW −446.06 Da), alkaline phosphatase (ALP) (9001-78-9), ATP (34369-07-8, MW − 551.14 Da), adenosine monophosphate (AMP) (18422-05-4, MW −365.24 Da), adenine (73-24-5, MW-135.13 Da), cytidine (65-46-3, MW − 243.22 Da), guanine (73-40-5, MW − 151.13 Da), bovine serum albumin (BSA) (9048-46-8, MW - ∼69,324 Da), glutathione (GSH) (70-18-8, MW − 307.33 Da), thiamine (67-03-8, MW – 265.36 Da), all metal salts, terephthaldehyde (623-27-8, MW − 134.13 Da) and cysteine (52-90-4, MW − 121.16 Da) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Adenosine-5-diphosphate disodium salt (ADP) (16178-48-6, MW − 471.17 Da) was purchased from SRL, Pvt, Ltd, India.

The absorption spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs were acquired at room temperature on Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA). The fluorescence spectra were measured using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Oxidation state of Mo was investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) using K-alpha+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The High resolution - transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were collected with help of HR-TEM instrument (JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan).

First, TPA-Cys Schiff base was prepared by the reaction between TPA and Cys using microwave condition [8]. In detail, 1 mM (134.13 mg) of TPA was taken in a 25 mL round bottom flask and dissolved in 20 mL of ethanol. Then, 2 mM (242.32 mg) of Cys was added and continuously stirred at room temperature for 2 min. This reaction mixture was placed in a microwave reactor for 5 min (30-sec interval) at 150 W, resulting to form yellowish solid product, which confirms the formation of TPA-Cys Shiff base. The formed product was dried and recrystallized with ethanol (99.9%). This yellowish solid powder was further used for the synthesis of TPA-Cys-MoNCs.

Blue fluorescent TPA-Cys-MoNCs were prepared via one-step synthetic approach. Briefly, the as-synthesized yellowish solid Schiff base of TPA-Cys (10.20 mg, 6 mM) was dissolved into 5 mL of deionized water in a round bottom flask, and then MoCl3 (1 mL, 4.94 mM) solution was added. The reaction mixture was refluxed for 3.0 h at 70 °C, yielding to form TPA-Cys-MoNCs solution and the formed TPA-Cys-MoNCs solution was kept at 4 °C for further characterization and analytical applications.

For the detection of PPi, 500 µL of various concentrations of PPi (0.01–200 µM) was added into 1.0 mL of TPA-Cys-MoNCs and the solutions were vortexed for 120 s. Then, the emission spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs-PPi solutions were measured by exciting them at 340 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs without PPi (I0) and with PPi (I) were used to calculate the ratio for the construction of calibration graph for quantitative assay of PPi.

Quantum yield (QY) of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was measured with the help of quinine sulfate as a reference standard dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4. The following equation was used for the estimation of QY.
Q
Y

o

s

s
×
F
M
o
N
C
s
F
Q
s
×
η
M
o
N
C
s
2
η
Q
s
2
×
Q
Y
Q
s
where QYMONCs = QY of sample (TPA-Cys-MoNCs), AQs = Absorbance of reference, AMoNCs = Absorbance of TPA-Cys-MoNCs, FMoNCs = Fluorescence emission area of TPA-Cys-MoNCs, FQs = Fluorescence emission area of reference, ηMoNCs = Refractive index of TPA-Cys-MoNCs and ηQs = Refractive index of reference.

The application of TPA-Cys-MoNCs-based fluorescence approach was applied for the detection of PPi in real samples (urine and plasma). Healthy male and female urine and plasma samples were collected from Iyyer Pathology Laboratory, Surat, Gujarat, India. The collected (male and female) urine and plasma samples were diluted and spiked with different concentrations of PPi (10, 20 and 30 nM) separately. Then, 0.5 mL of the above solution was added into 1.0 mL of TPA-Cys-MoNCs solution, and measured the emission intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNC at 455 nm. The amount of PPi was estimated by the aforesaid procedure.

In the present work, the ligand TPA-Cys Schiff base was synthesized by the reaction between aldehyde group of TPA and amino group of Cys, which improves the stability of TPA-Cys-MoNCs by encapsulating Mo with TPA-Cys Schiff base. Since TPA-Cys Schiff base contains surface-active imine (CHN), thiol (SH) and hydroxyl (OH) group [8,21], which offers a highly reactive surface for effective encapsulation of Mo3+ ion for controlled reduction and stabilization of Mo in MoNCs (Scheme 1 ). To determine the surface chemistry of TPA-Cys-MoNCs, FT-IR spectra were studied for the characterization of fluorescent TPA-Cys-MoNCs. Fig. S1a in Supporting Information shows the FT-IR spectrum of pure TPA. The peaks at 1695, 2864 and 2760 cm−1 belong to CHO stretching and the peaks at 1500 and 1400 cm−1 correspond to CC aromatic stretching. The peak at 1201 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching. Fig. S1b in Supporting Information displays the FT-IR spectrum of pure Cys. The peaks at 3432, 3180, 2552 and 1618 cm−1 are ascribed to OH, NH2 asymmetric, SH, and CO stretchings, respectively. The peaks around 1429 to 1292 cm−1 represent CO/CN stretching of Cys. Fig. S1c in Supporting Information shows the FT-IR spectrum of TPA-Cys Schiff base. The peaks at 3418 and 2603 cm−1 are attributed due to OH and -SH stretchings, respectively. The characteristic peak at 1613 cm−1 (CHN stretching) confirms the formation of TPA-Cys Schiff base. Fig. S2a in Supporting Information displays FT-IR spectrum of TPA-Cys-MoNCs. The characteristic peaks at 3421 cm−1 (OH stretching), 2550–2620 cm−1 (SH stretching), and 1630–1600 cm−1 (CHN stretching) were slightly shifted or decreased the intensities due to the formation of TPA-Cys-MoNCs.

Fig. 1 demonstrates the UV–visible, excitation, and emission spectral characteristics of TPA-Cys-MoNCs. In the UV–visible spectrum of TPA-Cys-MoNCs, the maximum absorption peak was found at 330 nm, TPA-Cys-MoNCs did not exhibit any surface plasmon resonance peak in the visible range, confirming the formation of fluorescent TPA-Cys-MoNCs. When excited at 340 nm, TPA-Cys-MoNCs showed a spectacular emission peak at 455 nm, suggesting the formation of blue fluorescent MoNCs. The as-prepared TPA-Cys-MoNCs exhibited a clear yellowish color solution in daylight but showed a strong blue emission under UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm (Inset of Fig. 1).

For the fabrication of TPA-Cys-MoNCs with higher QY, the effect of TPA-Cys Schiff base ligand concentrations (8.82, 17.64 and 35.29 mg dissolved in 1.0 mL of water separately, 3–12 mM) and reaction time (1–4 h) were investigated on the spectral characteristics of TPA-Cys-MoNCs (Fig. S4a-b in Supporting Information). The emission peak at 455 nm was progressively increased with increasing concentration of TPA-Cys Schiff base from 3 to 6.0 mM after that the emission intensity decreased, suggesting 6.0 mM of TPA-Cys Schiff base was the optimum concentration for the synthesis of TPA-Cys-MoNCs (Fig. S4a in Supporting Information). In addition, the influence of reaction time on the emission spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was studied and depicted in Fig. S4b in Supporting Information. It was observed that the fluorescence emission spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs continuously enhanced as the reaction time increased from 1 to 3.0 h, there was no change in the emission spectra after 3.0 h, thus, 3.0 h was chosen as the optimum reaction time for the fabrication of TPA-Cys-MoNCs.

To investigate the maximum emission peak, steady-state emission spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs were studied under different excitation wavelengths of 310 to 390 nm (Fig. S5 in Supporting Information). These emission spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs showed that the emission intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was increased with increasing excitation wavelength from 310 to 340 nm and then the emission intensity decreased when excited from 340 to 390 nm. The maximum emission intensity was observed at 455 nm when excitated at 340 nm. Thus, the excitation wavelength of 340 nm was selected as optimum excitation wavelength for the analytical applications of TPA-Cys-MoNCs. The QY of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was found to be 9.02%, and TPA-Cys-MoNCs exhibited good stability over 75 days (Fig. S6a in Supporting Information).

The as-fabricated TPA-Cys-MoNCs was used as a probe for the detection of PPi. To examine the sensing ability of fluorescent TPA-Cys-MoNCs, 1.0 mL of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was taken into 2.0 mL glass vials and then 500 µL of biomolecules (PPi, ALP, ATP, AMP, adenine, cytidine, guanine, BSA, GSH and thymine, 1 mM) were added separately. Only PPi quenched the fluorescence intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs at 455 nm where as others did not cause any changes in the emission peak. Fig. 4 shows the fluorescence intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs with different biomolecules. To investigate the time for maximum quenching, the fluorescence emission spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs were studied upon the addition of PPi at different time intervals (30 – 600 s) (Fig. S7 in Supporting Information). These results demonstrated that the maximum quenching of emission intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was achieved within 120 s, which confirm the rapidity of probe for assaying of PPi. To investigate the stability of TPA-Cys-MoNCs, the stability of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was investigated by monitoring the emission spectra at different time intervals (1 – 75 days) (Fig. S6a in Supporting Information). After day 1, there was a slight decrease (∼9%) in the emission intensity and then the emission intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was almost unchanged upto 45 days. However, the emission intensity was dropdown to 50 and 25% at 60 and 75 days, suggesting TPA-Cys-Shiff base acts as good stabilizing agent for the fabrication of TPA-Cys-MoNCs with well dispersability and good stability. Further, the sensing ability of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was investigated towards PPi after 75 days (Fig. S6b in Supporting Information), showing a remarkable quenching ability even after 75 days, which implies that as-fabricated TPA-Cys-MoNCs acted as good fluorescent sensor for the recognition of PPi.

Furthermore, the pH of the solution has shown significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs. Briefly, the emission peak intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was investigated without and with of PPi by using phosphate buffer saline (PBS) pH (2.0–12.0). The fluorescence intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was studied after the addition of PPi at various pH. As shown in Fig. S8 in Supporting Information, we tested the fluorescence intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs with the addition of PPi at different PBS pH ranging from 2.0 to 12.0, showing almost similar quenching abilities at all PBS pHwhile the similar fluorescence quenching was also noticed without addition of PBS pH. In the case of pH 12 the fluorescence of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was slightly quenching with and without PPi due to the high base medium. These results demonstrated that PBS pH did not affect on the quenching of fluorescence intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs by PPi. Thus, TPA-Cys-MoNCs-based fluorescence assay of PPi was carried out without use of any buffer pH.

The possible sensing mechanism was proposed with help of FT-IR, lifetime, HR-TEM and absorption spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs with the introduction of PPi. Fig. S2b in Supporting Information shows the FT-IR spectrum of PPi, exhibiting the peaks at 1152–1125 and 907 cm−1 which correspond to stretching of PO3 2- and P-O-P [37]. However, these two peaks intensities were decreased and shifted at 1123 and 924 cm−1 for − PO3 2- and P-O-P stretching, respectively due to the interaction of PPi with TPA-Cys-MoNCs (Fig. S2c in Supporting Information). Also, the absorbance of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was drastically decreased with the addition of PPi (Fig. S9 in Supporting Information), confirming the formation of TPA-Cys-MoNCs-PPi networks, which results the aggregation of MoNCs. Furthermore, the dispersed state of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was changed due to the aggregation state with the addition of PPi (Fig. 2c). The average size of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was increased to 7.99 ± 0.88 nm after the addition of PPi (Fig. 2e), implying the TPA-Cys-MoNCs aggregation was occurred when PPi introduced into TPA-Cys-MoNCs. To better understand the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was studied in the presence and absence of PPi (Fig. S10a- b in Supporting Information). The average fluorescence lifetime of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was found at 2.09 ns (obtained using 3 exponential decay fit, τ1 = 1.64, τ2 = 1.92 and τ3 = 2.71 ns) (Fig. S10a in Supporting Information), however it was decreased to 1.77 ns (obtained using 3 exponential decay fit, τ1 = 0.23, τ2 = 1.29 and τ3 = 3.80 ns) in the presence of PPi (Fig. S10b in Supporting Information), revealing the dynamic quenching mechanism between TPA-Cys-MoNCs and PPi [38]. The HR-TEM and FT-IR results strongly supported that the fluorescence quenching of TPA-Cys-MoNCs by PPi is due to the formation of TPA-Cys-MoNCs-PPi aggregates.

To investigate the sensitivity of the probe, the emission spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs were investigated by introducing different concentrations of PPi ranging from 0.010 to 200 µM. In this study, 0.5 mL of different concentrations of PPi was added separately into 1 mL of TPA-Cys-MoNCs solution. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the emission spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs showed fluorescence quenching after the addition of PPi. It was observed that the introduction of PPi to TPA-Cys-MoNCs was progressively promoted the fluorescence quenching at 455 nm. The emission intensity ratio at 455 nm (I0/I, I0 is the intensity without PPi and I is the intensity with PPi) was linear with the concentration of PPi (0.010–200 µM), exhibiting a linear equation I0/I = 1.1504 [PPi] (nM) + 1.0428 (n = 3, R2 of 0.9919) (Fig. 5b). The detection limit (LOD) of PPi was found to be 0.9 nM by calculating 3σ/slope, where “σ” represents the standard deviation of the blank. Importantly, the bright blue fluorescence of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was gradually decreased as concentration of PPi increased (Inset of Fig. 5a) when irradiated under 365 nm of UV light. The analytical significance of the probe was compared with other reported analytical techniques [39–61], demonstrating that the present method exhibited superior analytical merits and a lower detection limit for PPi (Table 1 ).

The detection capability of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was examined in the presence of interfering chemical species. Briefly, the effect of various interfering chemical species (aminoacids- alanine, aspartic acid, glutamic acid, phenylalanine; similar phosphorus containing molecules - ADP, ALP, AMP, ATP; metal ions- Hg2+, Ba2+, Cr3+, Zn2+ and Fe3+; andanions- Cr2O7 2−, SO4 2−, S2−, Cl−, Br−, I− 0.5 mM) was investigated upon the addition into TPA-Cys-MoNCs solution with and without PPi (Figs. S11-S12 in Supporting Information). The addition of the above co-existence chemical substances did not show any obvious effect on the emission spectral characteristics of TPA-Cys-MoNCs, however the emission spectra of TPA-Cys-MoNCs was remarkably quenched only with the introduction of PPi (10 μM) even in the presence of other interfering chemical species. Figs. 4 and S11 in Supporting Information confirms the good selectivity of TPA-Cys-MoNCs system for assaying of PPi in the presence of co-existence of several congeners.

The developed probe was applied for assaying of PPi in biofluids (urine and plasma). Biofluids (urine and plasma) were collected from Iyyer Pathology Laboratory, Surat, Gujarat, India. The collected plasma sample was centrifuged for 10 min at 7000 rpm and then diluted 10 times. The diluted samples were spiked with different concentrations of PPi (10, 20 and 30 nM). For quantification of PPi, 0.5 mL (10, 20 and 30 nM, PPi) of above solution was added to 1.0 mL of TPA-Cys-MoNCs solution separately, and then measured the emission intensity of TPA-Cys-MoNC at 455 nm. As shown in Table 2 , the spiked concentrations of PPi were in good agreement with the measured values and the recovery data of TPA-Cys-MoNCs as a fluorescent probe in plasma and urine were 96.22 – 99.28% and 97.29 – 99.58%, respectively with the relative standard deviation (RSD) values < 1.0 (n = 3). These results revealed that the as-fabricated TPA-Cys-MoNCs-based fluorescence approach could be used as promising analytical platform for assaying of PPi in biofluids.

In summary, blue emissive TPA-Cys-MoNCs were successfully synthesized by using TPA-Cys Schiff base as a ligand. The as-synthesized TPA-Cys-MoNCs showed emission peak at 455 nm when excited at 340 nm and blue fluorescence under a UV lamp at 365 nm. The as-synthesized TPA-Cys-MoNCs are thoroughly well characterized by various analytical techniques such as absorption, FT-IR and fluorescence spectroscopy, HR-TEM and XPS. Based on the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of TPA-Cys-MoNCs in the presence of PPi, a novel fluorescence analytical strategy was developed for the sensitive detection of PPi due to the aggregation of TPA-Cys-MoNCs induced by PPi and dynamic quenching. The as-synthesized TPA-Cys-MoNCs-based fluorescent sensor delivers a simple analytical strategy for assaying of PPi in biological fluids with superior selectivity sensitivity. Taking advantage of the superior analytical features, we believe that TPA-Cys-MoNCs-based fluorescence assay could provide a simple analytical strategy for the quantification of PPi in biological samples (urine and plasma).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India (EMR/2016/002621/IPC). MK sincerely acknowledges the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India for providing the doctoral fellowship to carry this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121536.

ce:section.ce:para:

Ouyang, Plasmon-Enhanced Fluorescence-Based Core-Shell Gold Nanorods as a Near-IR Fluorescent Turn-On Sensor for the Highly Sensitive Detection of Pyrophosphate in Aqueous Solution, Adv. Funct. Mater. 25 (2015) 7017–7027. doi:10.1002/adfm.201503326.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On-off-on luminescent pyrophosphate probe based on the use of Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots and using Eu(III) as a mediator
Microchim. Acta.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Pang, Y. Lu, X. Gao, P. Song, F. Yang, Y. Liu, On-off-on luminescent pyrophosphate probe based on the use of Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots and using Eu(III) as a mediator, Microchim. Acta. 185 (2018). doi:10.1007/s00604-018-3003-3.
